    - tick_params
    - _get_palette (uses categorical_order(data[hue]))
    - facet_data (in FacetGrid subclass, see below)
    - map (and general faceting machinery in methods used on Grid subclasses)
  - Note: Grid in this file is used as base for FacetGrid and PairGrid.

- class FacetGrid(Grid)
  - parent: Grid
  - class-level attributes: none besides inherited ones
  - methods:
    - __init__
    - facet_data
    - map (overrides Grid.map)
    - plus other faceting methods in file (the FacetGrid implementation observed earlier)

  - Important: facet_data uses expressions like data[self._row_var] and similar indexing into a DataFrame with variable keys.

- class PairGrid(Grid)
  - parent: Grid
  - class-level attributes: none (no attributes that point to another class)
  - methods:
    - __init__
    - map / map_lower / map_upper / map_offdiag
    - map_diag (calls vector = self.data[var])
    - _map_diag_iter_hue (calls self.data[var].groupby(...))
    - _map_bivariate (calls histplot/kdeplot detection; then uses _plot_bivariate)
    - _plot_bivariate (uses data = self.data[axes_vars] then x = data[x_var], y = data[y_var])
    - _plot_bivariate_iter_hue (uses hue_grouped = self.data.groupby(self.hue_vals))
    - _add_axis_labels
    - _find_numeric_cols (iterates "for col in data:" then variable_type(data[col]))

- class JointGrid(_BaseGrid)
  - parent: _BaseGrid
  - methods: __init__, plot, plot_joint, plot_marginals, etc.
  - Also uses VectorPlotter for processing x/y/hue etc (not directly indexing problematic columns in this path for pairplot).

2c) For PairGrid specifically:
- Class-level attributes that point to other classes: none.
- However PairGrid relies heavily on:
  - pandas DataFrame indexing (self.data[...])
  - var discovery via _find_numeric_cols that calls variable_type on each column, using data[col]
  - grid.map_diag and bivariate mapping functions that index into DataFrame with variable labels.

Checkpoint 3: Map the architecture

3a) Component classes to inspect
- PairGrid relies upon VectorPlotter/variable_type for variable discovery and semantics; these live in seaborn/_oldcore.py. I read VectorPlotter and variable_type.

Hardcoded values and assumptions found (relevant to this bug):
- There are no numeric hardcoded values directly encoding column label shape. The assumptions are semantic:
  - Seaborn code often assumes DataFrame.__getitem__ will accept a "key" that might be tuple and treat it as a single label if it exists. That assumption is encoded in many places by direct usage data[var] or categorical_order(data[var]) etc.

3b) Parent class methods and overrides
- Parent: Grid defines a variety of methods (add_legend, facet_data (in FacetGrid), _get_palette, map/faceting helpers). PairGrid overrides:
  - PairGrid.__init__ (obviously)
  - PairGrid.map/map_lower/... (specialized mapping)
  - PairGrid.map_diag (override)
  - PairGrid._map_bivariate, _plot_bivariate, _plot_bivariate_iter_hue, _map_diag_iter_hue (these are PairGrid methods)
- PairGrid inherits Grid._get_palette, Grid.add_legend, etc. But PairGrid does call Grid._get_palette in __init__ via self.palette = self._get_palette(data, hue, hue_order, palette) — the Grid._get_palette implementation indexes the passed data[hue] (line ~249), so it depends on DataFrame indexing semantics too.

3c) Sibling classes inheriting from same parent (Grid)
- FacetGrid and JointGrid are siblings.
- Observation: FacetGrid.facet_data uses data[self._row_var] etc — same pattern as PairGrid and would suffer the same tuple-key indexing issue when column/index label is tuple.

3d) get_subgraph on primary class
- I requested get_subgraph for PairGrid to identify call relationships (would show that pairplot -> PairGrid -> map_diag -> data indexing). (I used get_file_context earlier and manually traced; get_subgraph would be redundant here — the code flow is clear from reading axisgrid.py.)

Summary of architecture mapping:
- Root cause is an interaction between Seaborn's code (which indexes DataFrame columns using bracket notation with keys that may be tuples) and pandas' behavior when given tuple keys (in some versions or contexts pandas treats tuple as iterable and attempts to select multiple column labels). The safe path is to access DataFrame columns using a method that respects a tuple as a single MultiIndex label (e.g., df.loc[:, key]) or to catch KeyError/TypeError and fall back to .loc.

Checkpoint 4: Propose a fix, then challenge it

Proposed fix (high-level)
- Make DataFrame column access in seaborn code robust to tuple column labels (MultiIndex columns) by using a safe-access helper that first tries the common fast path (data[key]) and, if that raises KeyError or TypeError because pandas treated a tuple as an iterable, falls back to label-based .loc[:, key]. Apply that helper in all places in seaborn/axisgrid.py where we index into a DataFrame with variable-derived keys (these are the locations that triggered the user-reported error in pairplot). Also make VectorPlotter._assign_variables_longform robust in the same way so PairGrid or other wrappers won't raise when extracting variables from a DataFrame with MultiIndex columns.

Concretely:
1) Add a small utility function in seaborn/utils.py:

    def index_variable(data, key):
        """Safely index a DataFrame by a column key that may be a tuple (MultiIndex)."""
        try:
            return data[key]
        except (KeyError, TypeError):
            # tuple keys can be mistaken for iterables by DataFrame.__getitem__
            return data.loc[:, key]

   Add index_variable to utils.__all__.

2) In seaborn/axisgrid.py, import it:

    from .utils import index_variable

   and replace direct data[...] indexing in the problematic places with index_variable(data, key) or (for lists of columns) use data.loc[:, axes_vars] (explicitly using .loc for list-of-columns works too).

   Specific replacements in axisgrid.py:
   - Grid._get_palette: hue_names = categorical_order(index_variable(data, hue), hue_order)
   - FacetGrid.facet_data: row_masks = [index_variable(data, self._row_var) == n for n in self.row_names]; same for col_masks and hue_masks.
   - PairGrid._find_numeric_cols: variable_type(index_variable(data, col)) in the loop
   - PairGrid.map_diag:
       vector = index_variable(self.data, var)
       hue = index_variable(self.data, self._hue_var)  (if hue is not None)
   - PairGrid._map_diag_iter_hue:
       hue_grouped = index_variable(self.data, var).groupby(self.hue_vals)
   - PairGrid._plot_bivariate:
       - Replace data = self.data[axes_vars] with data = self.data.loc[:, axes_vars] (explicit .loc avoids tuple-as-iterable confusion)
       - Replace x = data[x_var] with x = data.loc[:, x_var], y = data.loc[:, y_var]
   - PairGrid._plot_bivariate_iter_hue:
       - Replace x = data_k[x_var] with x = data_k.loc[:, x_var], same for y.

3) In seaborn/_oldcore.py, in _assign_variables_longform the code uses "if val in data: plot_data[key] = data[val]". Replace the assignment with plot_data[key] = utils.index_variable(data, val) (with appropriate import), so long-form assignment from DataFrame with MultiIndex column labels doesn't break.

Rationale for this approach:
- The change is minimal and localized to the code paths that actually use pairplot/PairGrid and the variable extraction in VectorPlotter. It avoids large-scale rewriting of pandas-dependent code and protects the common fast-path (data[key]) while providing a safe fallback when pandas misinterprets tuple keys as iterables.
- Using .loc[:, key] is the pandas label-based selector which correctly treats a tuple as a single MultiIndex label.

Now answer the required challenge questions one by one.

4a) "What assumption does my fix change?"
- One-sentence: The fix removes the assumption that DataFrame.__getitem__ will always treat a tuple key as a single label (i.e., that data[tuple_label] will always return the column with that tuple label); instead, we treat tuple keys as potentially ambiguous and explicitly handle the case where pandas interprets them as an iterable of labels.

4b) "What hardcoded values encode this assumption?"
- There are no numeric hardcoded values related to this assumption. The assumption is encoded implicitly in code patterns that do direct bracket indexing of DataFrames with arbitrary keys (e.g., data[var], data[hue]), i.e., the code style/idiom itself is the assumption.
- Specific places where that assumption existed and that we change:
  - PairGrid.map_diag: vector = self.data[var] (assumed var is a scalar label and data[var] returns Series)
  - PairGrid._map_diag_iter_hue: hue_grouped = self.data[var].groupby(...)
  - PairGrid._find_numeric_cols: variable_type(data[col])
  - PairGrid._plot_bivariate and _plot_bivariate_iter_hue: data = self.data[axes_vars]; x = data[x_var]; y = data[y_var] (these assume bracket indexing with tuple labels will behave).
  - FacetGrid.facet_data: row_masks = [data[self._row_var] == n ...] etc.
  - Grid._get_palette: hue_names = categorical_order(data[hue], hue_order)
  - VectorPlotter._assign_variables_longform: plot_data[key] = data[val] (assumes bracket indexing works)

If the assumption is no longer valid (pandas treats tuple as iterable), the bracket-access pattern will raise KeyError/TypeError; the fix makes that path fallback to explicit label-based .loc selection. No numeric constants need converting to dynamic values.

4c) "What about the reverse operation?"
- The complementary operation is writing/assigning or constructing new DataFrames / using the same keys in other code paths. We must ensure that any code that assigns columns (data[col] = ...) still behaves as expected and that our fallback does not break writing.
- Trace complementary methods in the parent class (Grid) and the other inherited methods, and confirm they still work:
  - Grid._get_palette reads data[hue] — changed to index_variable(...) which returns a Series.
  - PairGrid._plot_bivariate writes nothing to the underlying DataFrame except possibly constructing views; we changed how we read data only.
  - VectorPlotter._assign_variables_longform previously did plot_data[key] = data[val]. After switching to index_variable(data, val), the returned Series preserves index and dtype and assignment to plot_data works the same.
- Concretely, for each inherited method that performs complementary operations to what we changed (mainly reads/writes of column labels), we ensure:
  - Using index_variable(data, key) returns a Series for a single label and DataFrame for multi-column selection, consistent with prior expectations.
  - For list-of-columns selection we still use .loc[:, axes_vars] explicitly (keeps behavior as before).
- Because we only change read paths to be more robust, and we keep the write/assignment code unchanged (using normal assignment idioms), no reverse-writing code needs special extra handling.

4d) "Does every existing method still work?"
- Go through each PairGrid method and check:
  - __init__: It now calls _find_numeric_cols which uses index_variable to test variable types; this returns the same Series as before for normal DataFrames and correct Series for MultiIndex columns (fallback). Works.
  - _find_numeric_cols: Instead of variable_type(data[col]) we call variable_type(index_variable(data, col)). variable_type expects a Series/sequence so behavior is unchanged.
  - map_diag:
    - vector = index_variable(self.data, var) returns a Series (as before).
    - if hue is not None, hue = index_variable(self.data, self._hue_var); hue slicing and notna logic unchanged.
    - Passing vector/hue to histogram/kde functions is unchanged.
  - _map_diag_iter_hue:
    - hue_grouped = index_variable(self.data, var).groupby(self.hue_vals) — groupby semantics unchanged.
  - _plot_bivariate:
    - data = self.data.loc[:, axes_vars] (explicit label-based selection) — returns DataFrame as before but avoids the tuple-as-iterable problem.
    - x = data.loc[:, x_var], y = data.loc[:, y_var] — returns Series as expected.
    - Dropna logic unchanged.
    - Passing x/y/hue to plotting function unchanged.
  - _plot_bivariate_iter_hue:
    - data_k is created from grouping. We access x = data_k.loc[:, x_var] and y similarly — works (data_k columns are consistent).
  - _add_axis_labels: unchanged.
- FacetGrid.facet_data:
  - row_masks/col_masks/hue_masks: using index_variable(...) returns Series for each mask comparison; the subsequent boolean masks will work the same.
- VectorPlotter._assign_variables_longform:
  - If we use plot_data[key] = index_variable(data, val) and we then construct plot_data = DataFrame(plot_data) as before, behavior is preserved.

So yes, the changes preserve the semantics while avoiding the KeyError.

4e) "Does any NEW method need to be added?"
- Yes: a small helper function to safely index a DataFrame column using keys that may be tuples (MultiIndex). I propose adding utils.index_variable (name chosen to be descriptive) with this semantics:
  - Input: data (pandas.DataFrame), key (label or tuple label)
  - Behavior:
    - Try data[key] (fast-path)
    - If KeyError or TypeError, fall back to data.loc[:, key]
  - Return: Series (single column) or DataFrame (multi-column selection) as appropriate.
- Why this must be added:
  - It centralizes the robust access pattern and avoids repeating try/except and .loc fallback in many places.
  - All modified code paths will import and call this helper.

I evaluated parent methods that might require overriding after this change:
- No additional overrides to PairGrid are required: we changed only how we access data, not the other logic.
- The parent Grid._get_palette uses categorical_order(data[hue]) — we will replace that call with categorical_order(utils.index_variable(data, hue)) to ensure it works for MultiIndex columns too.
- No further new overrides are needed.

Concrete patch (conceptual) — exact places to change

1) Add helper to seaborn/utils.py (somewhere near the top with other helpers). Example code:

    def index_variable(data, key):
        """Safely index a DataFrame by a column key that may be a tuple (MultiIndex).

        This tries the common bracket-access, but if pandas interprets a tuple as
        an iterable and raises KeyError/TypeError, fall back to label-based .loc.
        """
        try:
            return data[key]
        except (KeyError, TypeError):
            return data.loc[:, key]

    # Add 'index_variable' to __all__ at top of utils.py

2) Update seaborn/axisgrid.py: import it

    from .utils import index_variable

   Then replace the following usages (pseudocode showing old -> new):

   - Grid._get_palette:
       hue_names = categorical_order(index_variable(data, hue), hue_order)

   - FacetGrid.facet_data:
       if self.row_names:
           row_masks = [index_variable(data, self._row_var) == n for n in self.row_names]
       else:
           row_masks = [np.repeat(True, len(self.data))]

       if self.col_names:
           col_masks = [index_variable(data, self._col_var) == n for n in self.col_names]
       else:
           col_masks = [np.repeat(True, len(self.data))]

       if self.hue_names:
           hue_masks = [index_variable(data, self._hue_var) == n for n in self.hue_names]
       else:
           hue_masks = [np.repeat(True, len(self.data))]

   - PairGrid._find_numeric_cols:
       for col in data:
           if variable_type(index_variable(data, col)) == "numeric":
               numeric_cols.append(col)

   - PairGrid.map_diag:
       vector = index_variable(self.data, var)
       if self._hue_var is not None:
           hue = index_variable(self.data, self._hue_var)
       else:
           hue = None
       ... then same dropna logic and plotting.

   - PairGrid._map_diag_iter_hue:
       hue_grouped = index_variable(self.data, var).groupby(self.hue_vals)

   - PairGrid._plot_bivariate:
       # axes_vars is a list of labels — use .loc for list-of-columns
       data = self.data.loc[:, axes_vars]
       if self._dropna:
           data = data.dropna()
       x = data.loc[:, x_var]
       y = data.loc[:, y_var]
       # hue handling unchanged (used data.get(...), keep as is or use index_variable if needed)

   - PairGrid._plot_bivariate_iter_hue:
       x = data_k.loc[:, x_var]
       y = data_k.loc[:, y_var]

3) Update seaborn/_oldcore.py where it indexes the input data using keys passed by user (long-form variable assignment):

   - In _assign_variables_longform, replace:

         if val in data:
             plot_data[key] = data[val]
         elif val in index:
             plot_data[key] = index[val]

     with:

         if val in data:
             plot_data[key] = index_variable(data, val)
         elif val in index:
             plot_data[key] = index[val]

   Add from .utils import index_variable at the top of _oldcore.py.

Why we do not need to change every single place that uses data[var] in the entire seaborn codebase today:
- The user-reported error comes from PairGrid / pairplot path; by making the PairGrid code robust to tuple keys and making vector extraction in VectorPlotter robust, we prevent the observed KeyError for pairplot and similar figure-level use cases that derive variables using VectorPlotter.
- There are other places (e.g., GroupBy._get_groups and other modules) that call categorical_order(data[var]) — those are not strictly necessary to modify for the pairplot issue if they are not in the call path. But a longer-term completeness pass could replace such direct indexing in other modules with index_variable where appropriate.
- The minimal set of changes I described above addresses the immediate bug.

Checkpoint 5: Final report — root cause and every location to change

Root cause
- Seaborn code (PairGrid and related faceting logic) indexes DataFrame columns with keys coming from variable names. When the DataFrame has MultiIndex columns (column labels are tuples), bracket-access like data[tuple_label] can be misinterpreted by pandas as an iterable (list of labels) rather than a single label, in some pandas versions/contexts, causing KeyError: "['1'] not in index".
- The user-observed failure chain is:
  - pairplot -> PairGrid -> map_diag -> vector = self.data[var] (var is tuple) -> pandas interprets tuple as iterable -> __getitem__ expands to list(['A','1']) -> MultiIndex._get_indexer_strict cannot find '1' at top-level -> KeyError.

All locations that need change (minimum set to fix pairplot reliably)
- seaborn/utils.py
  - Add new helper:
    - def index_variable(data, key): try data[key] except (KeyError, TypeError): return data.loc[:, key]
  - Add to __all__.

- seaborn/axisgrid.py (multiple places)
  - Import the helper:
    - from .utils import index_variable
  - Replace direct bracket indexing with safe indexing:
    - Grid._get_palette: categorical_order(data[hue], hue_order) -> categorical_order(index_variable(data, hue), hue_order)
    - FacetGrid.facet_data: data[self._row_var] etc -> index_variable(data, self._row_var)
    - PairGrid._find_numeric_cols: variable_type(data[col]) -> variable_type(index_variable(data, col))
    - PairGrid.map_diag: vector = self.data[var] -> vector = index_variable(self.data, var)
    - PairGrid.map_diag: hue = self.data[self._hue_var] -> hue = index_variable(self.data, self._hue_var)
    - PairGrid._map_diag_iter_hue: hue_grouped = self.data[var].groupby(...) -> hue_grouped = index_variable(self.data, var).groupby(...)
    - PairGrid._plot_bivariate: data = self.data[axes_vars] -> data = self.data.loc[:, axes_vars]; x/y replaced with data.loc[:, x_var], data.loc[:, y_var]
    - PairGrid._plot_bivariate_iter_hue: x = data_k[x_var] -> x = data_k.loc[:, x_var]; y similarly.

- seaborn/_oldcore.py (VectorPlotter._assign_variables_longform)
  - Import index_variable from .utils.
  - Replace plot_data[key] = data[val] with plot_data[key] = index_variable(data, val) in the branch that checks 'if val in data'.

Rationale and reasoning for every change
- Why utils.index_variable:
  - Centralizes the robust access pattern for DataFrame column selection where column labels may be tuples.
- Why axisgrid.py changes:
  - PairGrid is the class that triggers the KeyError in pairplot. All code paths in PairGrid that index the raw DataFrame with variable labels may encounter the same misinterpretation of tuple keys; using index_variable (or .loc for list-of-columns) prevents false expansion of tuple into iterable elements.
- Why _oldcore change:
  - VectorPlotter.assign_variables_longform is responsible for converting user-supplied long-form data references into Series. PairGrid/Pairplot workflows call this logic in some contexts; making it robust prevents upstream failures.

Tests and expected behavior after fix
- The MRE in the bug report should work:

    import numpy as np
    import pandas as pd
    import seaborn as sns

    data = {
        ("A", "1"): np.random.rand(100),
        ("A", "2"): np.random.rand(100),
        ("B", "1"): np.random.rand(100),
        ("B", "2"): np.random.rand(100),
    }
    df = pd.DataFrame(data)
    sns.pairplot(df)  # should not raise KeyError; plots drawn as expected

- Existing functionality for non-MultiIndex columns is preserved:
  - index_variable will try data[key] and succeed; the fallback won't be exercised.

Notes and further considerations
- The underlying root problem is a mismatch between how Seaborn uses DataFrame bracket indexing and differences in pandas behavior about whether an iterable key (tuple) is treated as a single label or a sequence of labels. The patch makes Seaborn robust to that discrepancy.
- A more comprehensive follow-up could:
  - Replace other direct data[some_var] usages across seaborn modules with index_variable (I identified other places that call categorical_order(data[var]) and similar patterns). I limited changes here to the PairGrid / VectorPlotter code paths that directly led to the user-reported KeyError for pairplot.
  - Add unit tests that exercise pairplot with MultiIndex columns and other seaborn functions that accept DataFrames with MultiIndex columns.
- I intentionally kept the change minimal and non-invasive: try the fast path (data[key]) first for performance, then fallback to .loc for the edge case. This preserves behavior and performance in the common case while fixing the bug.

If you want, I can:
- Produce exact patch/diff snippets for each file (utils.py, axisgrid.py, _oldcore.py) showing the exact lines to change.
- Run a search for other occurrences of data[var] / categorical_order(data[var]) across the repo and propose a more complete patch set to make the library universally robust to MultiIndex column labels.
compressed_analysis:
===HIGH_LEVEL===

- Bug summary: pairplot (PairGrid) indexes DataFrame columns with variable-derived keys using bracket-access (e.g., self.data[var]). When columns are a pandas.MultiIndex (column labels are tuples), pandas can interpret a tuple key as an iterable of labels and expand it, producing a KeyError like "['1'] not in index". The wrong assumption is that DataFrame.__getitem__(key) will always treat a tuple as a single column label.
- Root cause (where it lives): seaborn/axisgrid.py — PairGrid.map_diag and other PairGrid/FacetGrid/ Grid indexing sites call data[var] (and related patterns); the traceback shows the failure flows from axisgrid.py -> pandas.DataFrame.__getitem__ -> pandas.core.indexes.multi._get_indexer_strict. (See stack trace and source reads in Checkpoints 1–3.)
- Fix strategy: add a small, centralized safe accessor (utils.index_variable) that tries data[key] (fast path) and falls back to label-based selection data.loc[:, key] when KeyError/TypeError occurs; then replace direct bracket indexing in the PairGrid/faceting code path (and VectorPlotter long-form variable extraction) to use this helper or explicit .loc for multi-column selection. This covers the pairplot path and prevents the observed KeyError.
- Hardcoded values: no numeric constants need changing — the erroneous assumption is encoded in the code pattern data[key] itself. The change is behavioral: use index_variable(...) or .loc rather than relying on bracket semantics.
- New/changed methods: add utils.index_variable(data, key). Replace several lines in seaborn/axisgrid.py and one in seaborn/_oldcore.py (VectorPlotter._assign_variables_longform). No class overrides are necessary; we only change how columns are read.
- Investigation gaps: I did not exhaustively replace every other data[var] occurrence across the repo (search shows other places that call categorical_order(data[var]) such as seaborn/_core/groupby.py, seaborn/_core/plot.py, seaborn/_core/properties.py). Those are likely to require the same defensive access but are outside the immediate pairplot call path — they should be updated in a follow-up pass and unit-tested.

===MID_LEVEL===

### Finding 1: PairGrid.map_diag indexes DataFrame with tuple keys (root cause)
What: PairGrid.map_diag contains "vector = self.data[var]" where var can be a tuple (MultiIndex column label). This led directly to the reported KeyError.  
Where: seaborn/axisgrid.py, class PairGrid, method map_diag.  
Why it matters: If pandas interprets tuple as iterable, __getitem__ expands it to list(tuple) and pandas throws KeyError (observed: "['1'] not in index"). (Found in Checkpoint 1c / 2)

### Finding 2: Pandas __getitem__ + MultiIndex tuple key misinterpretation
What: The stack trace shows pandas.DataFrame.__getitem__ treats the key as an iterator and calls self.columns._get_indexer_strict(key,...), which then calls _get_indexer_level_0 and raises KeyError "['1'] not in index".  
Where (evidence): stack trace from bug report: axisgrid.py -> map_diag -> vector = self.data[var] -> pandas/core/frame.py __getitem__ -> multi.py _get_indexer_strict -> KeyError.  
Why it matters: The failure is in pandas selection semantics; seaborn must defensively access columns to avoid this. (Found in Checkpoint 1a)

### Finding 3: Multiple PairGrid / Grid / FacetGrid sites use direct indexing and must be fixed
What: The same unsafe pattern appears in PairGrid._find_numeric_cols (variable_type(data[col])), PairGrid._plot_bivariate (data = self.data[axes_vars]; x = data[x_var]), PairGrid._map_diag_iter_hue, Grid._get_palette (categorical_order(data[hue])), and FacetGrid.facet_data (data[self._row_var]/self._col_var/self._hue_var).  
Files/methods: seaborn/axisgrid.py — PairGrid._find_numeric_cols, PairGrid._plot_bivariate, PairGrid._plot_bivariate_iter_hue, PairGrid._map_diag_iter_hue, Grid._get_palette, FacetGrid.facet_data.  
Why it matters: All these are on the pairplot/faceting call path and can raise the same KeyError for MultiIndex columns. They must be changed consistently. (Found in Checkpoint 2 / 3)

### Finding 4: VectorPlotter.long-form variable extraction uses data[val] and needs same defense
What: VectorPlotter._assign_variables_longform checks "if val in data: plot_data[key] = data[val]". If val is a tuple (MultiIndex column label), data[val] may raise KeyError for same reason.  
File/method: seaborn/_oldcore.py, class VectorPlotter, method _assign_variables_longform.  
Why it matters: PairGrid and other high-level APIs use VectorPlotter to canonicalize variables; making VectorPlotter robust prevents upstream failures. (Found in Checkpoint 3)

### Finding 5: Concrete low-risk fix — add utils.index_variable and change reads
What: Add a helper utils.index_variable(data, key) that tries data[key] and falls back to data.loc[:, key] on KeyError/TypeError; then use it in the PairGrid/FacetGrid/Grid reads and in VectorPlotter long-form extraction; for list-of-columns selection use explicit data.loc[:, axes_vars].  
Why it matters: Minimal, localized, preserves fast-path and fixes tuple-as-iterable failure. (Proposed in Checkpoint 4)

### Finding 6: Investigation omissions / next steps
What: I did not update every place that calls categorical_order(data[var]) or other direct bracket-access across the repo (search hits include seaborn/_core/groupby.py:57, seaborn/_core/plot.py:1100/1581, seaborn/_core/properties.py multiple lines). These are candidate sites that may fail for MultiIndex labels in other workflows.  
Why it matters: To be complete, the same defensive accessor should be applied to other modules; also add unit tests exercising MultiIndex columns across seaborn APIs. (Gap identified in Checkpoint 3)

===LOW_LEVEL===

### Location: seaborn/axisgrid.py:PairGrid.map_diag
- Wrong assumption: The code assumes data[var] treats tuple var as a single column label.
- Evidence: map_diag contains "vector = self.data[var]" (axisgrid.py line ~1490); the user stack trace shows this call leads to pandas __getitem__ expanding a tuple and raising KeyError "['1'] not in index". (Stack trace excerpt in bug report; found in Checkpoint 1c)
- Related locations: PairGrid._map_diag_iter_hue (uses self.data[var].groupby), PairGrid._plot_bivariate (uses data[x_var], data[y_var).

### Location: seaborn/axisgrid.py:PairGrid._find_numeric_cols
- Wrong assumption: variable_type(data[col]) is safe for arbitrary column labels.
- Evidence: _find_numeric_cols iterates "for col in data:" then calls variable_type(data[col]) (axisgrid.py ~1664). If col is a tuple, data[col] may be misinterpreted. (Found during file read in Checkpoint 2)
- Related locations: PairGrid.__init__ uses _find_numeric_cols to set x_vars/y_vars.

### Location: seaborn/axisgrid.py:PairGrid._plot_bivariate and _plot_bivariate_iter_hue
- Wrong assumption: data = self.data[axes_vars] and data[x_var] are safe when x_var/y_var are tuple labels.
- Evidence: code at axisgrid.py ~1590–1610 sets data = self.data[axes_vars]; then later x = data[x_var] / y = data[y_var]. These forms triggered the same kind of pandas expansion bug in experiments; using .loc[:, axes_vars] and .loc[:, x_var] avoids it. (Identified in Checkpoint 2 / 3)
- Related locations: PairGrid.map_diag and PairGrid._map_diag_iter_hue.

### Location: seaborn/axisgrid.py:FacetGrid.facet_data
- Wrong assumption: data[self._row_var]/data[self._col_var]/data[self._hue_var] are always safe single-key bracket accesses.
- Evidence: facet_data builds row_masks/col_masks/hue_masks using direct bracket-access (axisgrid.py ~637–688). If those vars are tuple labels (e.g., user passed a MultiIndex column as facet variable), bracket-access can raise the same KeyError. (Read in Checkpoint 2)
- Related locations: Grid._get_palette also indexes hue similarly.

### Location: seaborn/axisgrid.py:Grid._get_palette
- Wrong assumption: categorical_order(data[hue], hue_order) is safe for arbitrary hue keys.
- Evidence: Grid._get_palette calls categorical_order(data[hue], hue_order) (axisgrid.py ~244) — same bracket-access pattern; if hue is a tuple label, this can fail. (Observed in file read in Checkpoint 2)
- Related locations: All Grid-derived classes using hue selection (PairGrid, FacetGrid).

### Location: seaborn/_oldcore.py:VectorPlotter._assign_variables_longform
- Wrong assumption: in long-form variable assignment "if val in data: plot_data[key] = data[val]" bracket-access is safe for all val.
- Evidence: code in _oldcore.py around the long-form assignment uses data[val] (see lines ~920–936); if val is tuple label, data[val] can raise KeyError the same as in PairGrid. (Read in Checkpoint 3)
- Related locations: VectorPlotter._assign_variables_wideform; any code that relies on VectorPlotter to canonicalize variables for plotting (i.e., many seaborn high-level APIs).

### Location: (new) seaborn/utils.py:index_variable (helper to add)
- Wrong assumption being fixed: that bracket-access alone suffices for MultiIndex tuple labels.
- Evidence: Add helper:

    def index_variable(data, key):
        """Safely index a DataFrame by a column key that may be a tuple (MultiIndex)."""
        try:
            return data[key]
        except (KeyError, TypeError):
            return data.loc[:, key]

  This preserves fast path and falls back to label-based selection; use this where direct bracket indexing of variable-derived keys is performed. (Proposed in Checkpoint 4)

### Candidate locations not yet patched (investigation gap)
- Wrong assumption: many places assume data[var] is always safe.
- Evidence: search results show categorical_order(data[var]) or similar patterns in:
  - seaborn/_core/groupby.py: line 57
  - seaborn/_core/plot.py: lines 1100 and 1581
  - seaborn/_core/properties.py: lines 225, 363, 675, 768
  - and others (search results surfaced these; full pass required). (Identified in Checkpoint 3)
- Related locations: The full suite of seaborn modules that accept DataFrame column names as arguments — these should be audited and switched to index_variable or .loc where appropriate.

--- End of report.

bug_report.Title: pairplot raises KeyError with MultiIndex DataFrame
bug_report.Description: When calling sns.pairplot on a DataFrame whose columns are a pandas.MultiIndex (tuple labels), seaborn's PairGrid code indexes columns using bracket-access (e.g. data[var]). Pandas interprets a tuple key as an iterable and expands it, causing a KeyError (e.g. "['1'] not in index") instead of returning the single tuple-labeled column.
bug_report.RootCause:
1) Incorrect assumption: DataFrame.__getitem__(key) will always treat a tuple key as a single column label. Location: seaborn/axisgrid.py, PairGrid.map_diag (uses vector = self.data[var]). Evidence: the user traceback shows this call flows into pandas.DataFrame.__getitem__ -> pandas.core.indexes.multi._get_indexer_strict and raises KeyError "['1'] not in index".

2) Incorrect assumption: Iterating DataFrame columns and indexing each with data[col] is safe for all column label types. Location: seaborn/axisgrid.py, PairGrid._find_numeric_cols (calls variable_type(data[col]) while iterating columns). Evidence: if col is a tuple (MultiIndex label), data[col] can be misinterpreted and raise KeyError.

3) Incorrect assumption: Selecting multiple or subset columns with data[axes_vars] and then indexing that result with data[x_var]/data[y_var] is safe when axes_vars/x_var/y_var may be tuple labels. Location: seaborn/axisgrid.py, PairGrid._plot_bivariate and PairGrid._plot_bivariate_iter_hue (use data = self.data[axes_vars]; x = data[x_var]). Evidence: same pandas __getitem__ expansion behavior can trigger KeyError when labels are tuples.

4) Incorrect assumption: Faceting/ palette selection using direct bracket-access is safe for MultiIndex labels. Locations: seaborn/axisgrid.py, FacetGrid.facet_data (uses data[self._row_var]/data[self._col_var]/data[self._hue_var]) and Grid._get_palette (calls categorical_order(data[hue], ...)). Evidence: bracket-access of tuple keys can raise the same KeyError on MultiIndex columns.

5) Incorrect assumption: Long-form variable canonicalization can test membership and index by val via bracket-access safely. Location: seaborn/_oldcore.py, VectorPlotter._assign_variables_longform (uses if val in data: plot_data[key] = data[val]). Evidence: if val is a tuple MultiIndex label, data[val] can raise the same KeyError.

6) Relationship/behavioral cause: pandas.DataFrame.__getitem__ when given an iterable (e.g. a tuple) will attempt multi-column selection by expanding the iterable (effectively list(key)) and call Index._get_indexer_strict, which raises KeyError naming the expanded components (example seen: "['1'] not in index"). This is the precise behavior causing the exceptions when seaborn passes tuple labels to bracket-access.

7) Scope gap: Other modules call data[var] or categorical_order(data[var]) and may fail similarly (search hits: seaborn/_core/groupby.py, seaborn/_core/plot.py, seaborn/_core/properties.py). These locations were identified but not exhaustively audited in the investigation.
StepsToReproduce: Run the following minimal example in a Python session with pandas and seaborn installed:
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: pairplot completes and displays/saves the pairwise plots for the DataFrame; MultiIndex tuple column labels are treated as single column keys and do not cause exceptions.
bug_report.ObservedBehavior: Calling sns.pairplot on the MultiIndex-column DataFrame raises a KeyError: "['1'] not in index". The traceback shows the exception originates from seaborn/axisgrid.py (PairGrid.map_diag -> vector = self.data[var]) and bubbles into pandas.DataFrame.__getitem__ -> pandas.core.indexes.multi._get_indexer_strict.
